title: Why Cows Are a Climate Problem

lines.text: Every time a cow digests food, it releases methane into the atmosphere.
Methane is a potent greenhouse gas — far more warming per molecule than carbon dioxide.
This process, called enteric fermentation, happens in the cow's digestive system.
Livestock farming is one of the significant sources of agricultural greenhouse gas emissions globally.
Scientists are exploring feed additives that can reduce the methane a cow produces.
Some farms are also capturing methane from manure to generate energy instead.
Reducing meat consumption is another lever individuals can pull to lower their footprint.
The challenge is balancing global food needs with the climate cost of how we produce it.
